moduleTitle: Service Procedure — Surface & Soil Identification (Method Selection)
summary: How to identify the floor or surface type and read its soil and condition on a school or childcare site, so you select a method, tool and child-safe product matched to what is actually in front of you — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip-and-seal, or external pressure washing — rather than forcing one approach onto everything.

steps.title: Confirm the surface category first
steps.detail: Before choosing a method, be clear about what you are on. School and childcare floor care falls into four families: carpet (a textile covering), tile and grout (a dense tile face over porous grout), vinyl and resilient floors (which may carry a sealer and finish), and external hard surfaces (concrete, pavers, soft-fall). Each family is cleaned differently, so identify the surface before you reach for a tool or a chemical.

steps.title: Carpet — identify fibre and construction
steps.detail: Carpet fibre and construction shape every later decision. Different fibres respond differently to heat, moisture and chemistry, and construction (cut pile, loop, density, backing) affects how soil is held and how quickly the carpet releases moisture. You do not need a chemistry textbook, but you must identify what is in front of you so you can match method and product to the carpet rather than forcing one approach onto everything. ANSI/IICRC S100 frames this as part of pre-inspection — a recognised standard of care, not law.

steps.title: Carpet — read the soil type and load
steps.detail: Soil is not one thing. It ranges from dry particulate soil (grit and dust tracked in on shoes — the bulk of what lands in a school or childcare carpet) through oily and greasy residues, water-soluble spills, and spots that need targeted treatment. A large share of dry particulate soil comes out with thorough vacuuming before any wet process. Reading the soil type tells you whether the answer is mechanical removal, the right chemistry, or both — and whether the soil load justifies deep extraction or a lighter maintenance clean.

steps.title: Tile and grout — identify tile type and grout porosity
steps.detail: Tile and grout look like one surface but behave like two. The tile face is usually dense and largely sealed, so soil sits on top and rinses away; grout is porous and traps greasy and particulate soil below the surface, which is why grout lines darken on an otherwise serviceable floor. Confirm the tile type and condition tolerate your intended tool and chemistry before you start. Greasy, oily soils on hard floors are typically tackled with alkaline chemistry.

steps.title: Vinyl and resilient — identify the existing finish and substrate
steps.detail: For vinyl and resilient floors, identify whether there is an existing sealer and finish to strip, and confirm the floor is genuinely resilient. Strip-and-seal is one of the highest-risk jobs in floor care — it uses caustic chemistry and leaves a wet, slippery surface — so identifying the finish and substrate up front drives both the method and the safety plan. The generic process is strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats with drying between coats; the specifics for each product come from its SDS and TDS, never from memory.

steps.title: External hard surfaces — read the surface for pressure washing
steps.detail: For pressure washing, read the surface before you pull the trigger. Hard, sound concrete tolerates higher pressure; sand-jointed or clay pavers can be displaced or pitted and their jointing sand washed out; soft-fall and rubberised play surfaces, painted line-marking, timber and anything coated or sealed are easily damaged and call for the lowest practical pressure or a different method. Match the nozzle to the surface — the widest fan that still does the job, kept moving at a consistent distance and angle.

steps.title: Test in an inconspicuous area
steps.detail: Whatever the surface, test your intended method, chemistry or pressure on a small, out-of-the-way patch first and check the result before committing to the whole area. For spotting, test in an inconspicuous area and work spots before a general clean. A test patch is the cheapest insurance against damaging a floor you cannot easily put right.

steps.title: Match method to surface, soil and the occupied setting
steps.detail: Now select the method by matching what you identified against how fast the space must reopen. For carpet, hot water extraction gives deeper soil removal but adds moisture and a longer return to service (suited to heavy soil when you have a drying window), while encapsulation is a low-moisture method with a faster return (suited to occupied classrooms, accepting a lighter clean). For tile and grout, pair the right chemistry with mechanical agitation and proper rinse and recovery. For vinyl, follow the strip / rinse-and-neutralise / dry / thin-coats sequence. For external surfaces, use the widest practical fan and keep the nozzle moving.

steps.title: Select a child-safe product matched to the job
steps.detail: Choose a lower-tox product suited to occupied, child-occupied spaces for routine work, and reserve stronger chemistry (such as a caustic stripper) for periodic deep-clean or holiday work scheduled outside child contact. Read the current SDS for each product so you understand its hazards and PPE, and dilute strictly per the label — no stronger to be sure, since over-concentration raises both the hazard and the residue. The Australian CCW/Actichem catalogue gives real anchors: presprays such as Clean Force (SDS AP453) and Extracta Pro (SDS AP457), spotters such as Bust (SDS AP496) and GumGone (SDS AP483), alkaline hard-floor cleaner Defender SV (SDS AP672), and the strip-and-seal line Instastrip (SDS AP400) / Power Strip (SDS AP404), Pre-Seal (SDS AP171), Instaseal (SDS AP420) and Instagloss (SDS AP422).

steps.title: Confirm Australian availability and a current SDS
steps.detail: Confirm the product and any 230 V equipment are available in Australia and that you hold a current SDS (reviewed within the last 5 years) for every chemical, held in your register. Where a catalogue item — for example some sealers or accessories — lacks a current SDS, obtain the current SDS from the supplier before use rather than inventing a code. Availability check: the Australian catalogue products and 230 V machines named here (Actichem/CCW range; the Polystar 13-inch orbital encapsulation scrubber, approximately 330 mm pad; the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head; and the Dirty Grout Demon brushes) were AU-available as at 8 July 2026 — re-confirm current supply before you quote.

steps.title: Decide sequencing and a conditions-based drying approach
steps.detail: Decide the order of work and how you will manage drying. Sequence rooms so each is treated, dried and reopened while you move to the next, and never quote a fixed dry time — drying depends on the fibre or surface, the moisture applied, airflow, temperature and humidity, so manage it by conditions and airflow on the day. Place periodic, higher-moisture work (hot water extraction, strip-and-seal) in deep-clean or holiday windows where the space can be closed and isolated.

steps.title: Record the identification and method decision
steps.detail: Record what you identified and what you chose: the surface (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external material), the soil type and load, the test-patch result, and the method, tool and product selected with its AP-code and current SDS. Note that dilution comes from the product label/SDS/TDS (not a fixed figure) and drying is conditions-based (not a fixed time), and record any slip-resistance target to be verified under AS 4663. This record feeds your method statement and the documentation a school or childcare contract expects.

safety: Before selecting any product, read its current SDS for hazards, PPE, ventilation and first aid, and recognise GHS pictograms (e.g. the corrosion diamond on a caustic stripper) as your cue to take PPE and ventilation seriously.

Match chemistry to the job: vinyl strippers are typically alkaline/caustic and warrant gloves, eye protection and ventilation; presprays, spotters and alkaline hard-floor cleaners each carry their own hazard profile.
For pressure washing, treat high-pressure water as an injury hazard — never point the lance at anyone, use the widest practical fan and a test patch, and wear eye, foot and hearing protection.
Confirm equipment runs on 230 V / 50 Hz via a 10 A GPO on an RCD/safety switch and is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval.
Dilute strictly per the product label/SDS/TDS — never stronger to be sure — since over-concentration raises both the hazard and the residue left behind.
Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) with your SDS.
childSafe: Choose lower-tox products suited to occupied, child-occupied spaces for routine work, and reserve the harshest chemistry (such as caustic strippers) for periodic deep-clean or holiday windows away from child contact.
Because safe re-entry timing is product-specific, never rely on a generic time — follow the product SDS/TDS and coordinate access with the centre; isolate and clearly sign wet or treated floors.

Match method to the occupancy window: encapsulation's faster return to service suits occupied classrooms, while hot water extraction and strip-and-seal need the closed windows of a deep-clean or holiday program.
Keep children and pets away from the work zone, equipment, leads and chemicals throughout, and manage cords so they are never a trip hazard in a child-occupied space.
standards: ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for identifying carpet fibre, construction and soil at pre-inspection — guidance, not law. No IICRC CECs are awarded and this is not an IICRC-accredited course.
GHS hazard classification and Safe Work Australia SDS conventions; model WHS Regulation 346 (register) and correct labelling, including decanted containers.

AS 4586 (slip resistance of new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (slip resistance of existing/in-service surfaces) where a chosen method changes the surface; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
AS/NZS 3760 (test-and-tag) for the electrical equipment selected.
NQS Quality Area 2 for child-safe product selection in childcare settings.
documentation: A record of each surface identified (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external surface type) and the soil type and load read on it.
The test-patch result for any method, chemistry or pressure before full application.
The method, tool and product selected for each surface, with the product's AP-code and a current SDS in the register — obtain the current SDS from the supplier where one is missing.
The dilution source noted as the product label/SDS/TDS (not a fixed figure), and the drying approach noted as conditions-based (not a fixed time).
Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663.
A dated Australian-availability note for the catalogue products and equipment selected.
contentBodyHtml:
Service Procedure — Surface & Soil Identification (Method Selection)

Choosing the right method starts with correctly identifying the surface in front of you and reading its soil and condition. This procedure takes you across the four service families a school or childcare contract expects — carpet, tile and grout, vinyl strip-and-seal, and external pressure washing — so you match method, tool and child-safe product to what is actually there rather than forcing one approach onto everything.

  1. Confirm the surface category first. School and childcare floor care falls into four families: carpet (a textile covering), tile and grout (a dense tile face over porous grout), vinyl and resilient floors (which may carry a sealer and finish), and external hard surfaces (concrete, pavers, soft-fall). Identify the surface before you reach for a tool or a chemical.
  2. Carpet — identify fibre and construction. Different fibres respond differently to heat, moisture and chemistry, and construction (cut pile, loop, density, backing) affects how soil is held and how quickly the carpet releases moisture. Identify what is in front of you so you can match method and product to the carpet. ANSI/IICRC S100 frames this as part of pre-inspection — a recognised standard of care, not law.
  3. Carpet — read the soil type and load. Soil ranges from dry particulate (grit and dust — the bulk of what lands in a school carpet) through oily and greasy residues, water-soluble spills, and spots needing targeted treatment. A large share of dry particulate comes out with thorough vacuuming first. The soil type tells you whether the answer is mechanical removal, chemistry, or both — and whether the load justifies deep extraction or a lighter maintenance clean.
  4. Tile and grout — identify tile type and grout porosity. The tile face is usually dense and sealed, so soil sits on top and rinses away; grout is porous and traps soil below the surface, which is why grout lines darken first. Confirm the tile type and condition tolerate your intended tool and chemistry. Greasy, oily soils on hard floors are typically tackled with alkaline chemistry.
  5. Vinyl and resilient — identify the existing finish and substrate. Identify whether there is an existing sealer and finish to strip and confirm the floor is genuinely resilient. Strip-and-seal is one of the highest-risk jobs — caustic chemistry, a wet and slippery surface — so identifying the finish and substrate drives both method and safety plan. The generic process is strip, rinse and neutralise, dry, then multiple thin sealer and finish coats with drying between coats; specifics come from each product's SDS and TDS.
  6. External hard surfaces — read the surface for pressure washing. Sound concrete tolerates higher pressure; sand-jointed or clay pavers can be displaced or pitted; soft-fall, rubberised play surfaces, painted line-marking, timber and coated surfaces are easily damaged and call for the lowest practical pressure or a different method. Match the nozzle to the surface — the widest fan that still does the job, kept moving at a consistent distance and angle.
  7. Test in an inconspicuous area. Test your intended method, chemistry or pressure on a small, out-of-the-way patch first and check the result before committing. Work spots before a general clean. A test patch is the cheapest insurance against damaging a floor you cannot easily put right.
  8. Match method to surface, soil and the occupied setting. For carpet, hot water extraction gives deeper soil removal but adds moisture and a longer return to service (heavy soil, with a drying window), while encapsulation is low-moisture with a faster return (occupied classrooms, a lighter clean). For tile and grout, pair chemistry with agitation and proper rinse and recovery. For vinyl, follow the strip / rinse-and-neutralise / dry / thin-coats sequence. For external surfaces, use the widest practical fan and keep the nozzle moving.
  9. Select a child-safe product matched to the job. Choose a lower-tox product for routine occupied work; reserve stronger chemistry (a caustic stripper) for periodic deep-clean or holiday work outside child contact. Read the current SDS and dilute strictly per the label. Real anchors from the Australian CCW/Actichem catalogue include presprays Clean Force (AP453) and Extracta Pro (AP457), spotters Bust (AP496) and GumGone (AP483), alkaline hard-floor cleaner Defender SV (AP672), and the strip-and-seal line Instastrip (AP400)/Power Strip (AP404), Pre-Seal (AP171), Instaseal (AP420) and Instagloss (AP422).
  10. Confirm Australian availability and a current SDS. Confirm the product and any 230 V equipment are available in Australia and that you hold a current SDS (within 5 years) for every chemical. Where an item lacks a current SDS, obtain it from the supplier before use — do not invent a code. Availability check: the catalogue products and 230 V machines named here (Actichem/CCW range; Polystar 13-inch orbital encapsulation scrubber, approximately 330 mm pad; Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head; Dirty Grout Demon brushes) were AU-available as at 8 July 2026 — re-confirm before you quote.
  11. Decide sequencing and a conditions-based drying approach. Sequence rooms so each is treated, dried and reopened while you move on, and never quote a fixed dry time — drying depends on the surface, the moisture applied, airflow, temperature and humidity. Place periodic, higher-moisture work in deep-clean or holiday windows where the space can be closed and isolated.
  12. Record the identification and method decision. Record the surface (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external material), the soil type and load, the test-patch result, and the method, tool and product selected with its AP-code and current SDS. Note dilution comes from the label/SDS/TDS and drying is conditions-based, and record any slip-resistance target to be verified under AS 4663. This record feeds your method statement and the documentation the contract expects.

Safety

  • Read each product's current SDS for hazards, PPE, ventilation and first aid; recognise GHS pictograms (e.g. the corrosion diamond on a caustic stripper) as your cue to take PPE and ventilation seriously.
  • Match chemistry to the job: vinyl strippers are typically alkaline/caustic and warrant gloves, eye protection and ventilation; presprays, spotters and alkaline hard-floor cleaners each carry their own hazard profile.
  • Treat high-pressure water as an injury hazard — never point the lance at anyone, use the widest practical fan and a test patch, and wear eye, foot and hearing protection.
  • Confirm equipment runs on 230 V / 50 Hz via a 10 A GPO on an RCD/safety switch and is within its AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag interval.
  • Dilute strictly per the label/SDS/TDS — never stronger to be sure. Keep Poisons Information 13 11 26 with your SDS.

Child-safe

  • Choose lower-tox products for routine occupied work; reserve the harshest chemistry for periodic deep-clean or holiday windows away from child contact.
  • Re-entry timing is product-specific — follow the SDS/TDS and coordinate access with the centre; isolate and clearly sign wet or treated floors.
  • Match method to the occupancy window: encapsulation's faster return suits occupied classrooms, while hot water extraction and strip-and-seal need closed deep-clean or holiday windows.
  • Keep children and pets away from the work zone, equipment, leads and chemicals, and manage cords so they are never a trip hazard.

Standards

  • ANSI/IICRC S100 (S100-2021) as a recognised standard of care for identifying carpet fibre, construction and soil — guidance, not law. No IICRC CECs are awarded and this is not an IICRC-accredited course.
  • GHS hazard classification and Safe Work Australia SDS conventions; model WHS Regulation 346 (register) and correct labelling, including decanted containers.
  • AS 4586 (new pedestrian surfaces) and AS 4663 (existing/in-service surfaces) where a chosen method changes the surface; SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended classifications.
  • AS/NZS 3760 (test-and-tag) for the electrical equipment selected.
  • NQS Quality Area 2 for child-safe product selection in childcare.

Document this

  • Each surface identified (fibre and construction for carpet; tile and grout condition; existing vinyl finish; external surface type) and the soil type and load read on it.
  • The test-patch result for any method, chemistry or pressure before full application.
  • The method, tool and product selected for each surface, with the product's AP-code and a current SDS in the register.
  • The dilution source noted as the label/SDS/TDS (not a fixed figure), and drying noted as conditions-based (not a fixed time).
  • Any slip-resistance target and the plan to verify it under AS 4663.
  • A dated Australian-availability note for the catalogue products and equipment selected.
